questions.questionText: Viruses differ from bacteria in that viruses:
questions.optionB: Contain EITHER DNA or RNA (not both), are obligate intracellular parasites, and lack metabolic machinery
questions.explanation: Viruses: acellular, obligate intracellular parasites. Contain EITHER DNA OR RNA (never both — exception: cytomegalovirus carries mRNA). No ribosomes, no metabolic enzymes (depend on host cell machinery). Cannot be cultured on artificial media (require living cells — cell culture, embryonated eggs, animals). Not affected by antibiotics. Size: 20-300 nm (smaller than bacteria). Structure: nucleic acid core + protein capsid ± lipid envelope. Taxonomy: based on nucleic acid type, symmetry, envelope. Reference: Ananthanarayan — General Virology.

questions.questionText: The only DNA virus that replicates in the cytoplasm is:
questions.optionB: Poxvirus (has its own DNA-dependent RNA polymerase)
questions.explanation: DNA viruses generally replicate in the nucleus (use host RNA polymerase for transcription). EXCEPTION: Poxvirus — replicates in CYTOPLASM (carries its own DNA-dependent RNA polymerase, so doesn't need nuclear enzymes). Poxviridae: variola (smallpox — eradicated 1980), vaccinia (vaccine strain), molluscum contagiosum. Largest and most complex virus. Brick-shaped. Another mnemonic exception for RNA viruses: Retrovirus (HIV) and influenza replicate in the nucleus (retroviruses use reverse transcriptase → integrase). Reference: Ananthanarayan — Poxvirus.

questions.questionText: Viral culture is most commonly done using:
questions.optionB: Cell culture (tissue/cell lines such as Vero, HeLa, MRC-5) — with cytopathic effect (CPE) as indicator
questions.explanation: Virus isolation: (1) Cell culture (most common): monolayer cell cultures — primary (first passage from tissue), diploid (finite passages — MRC-5 for CMV), continuous (immortalized — Vero for poliovirus, HeLa for many viruses). Viral growth detected by: cytopathic effect (CPE), hemadsorption, interference, plaque assay. (2) Embryonated eggs: influenza (allantoic cavity), poxvirus (CAM). (3) Animal inoculation (rarely). Viruses CANNOT grow on artificial bacteriological media. Reference: Ananthanarayan — Virus Culture.

questions.questionText: ELISA (Enzyme-Linked Immunosorbent Assay) is used in virology for:
questions.optionB: Detection of viral antigens or antibodies (screening test — e.g., HIV screening, HBsAg detection)
questions.explanation: ELISA: immunoassay using enzyme-linked antibody → substrate conversion → color change (measured by spectrophotometer). Types: (1) Direct/Indirect: detect antibodies (e.g., HIV screening — anti-HIV Ab). (2) Sandwich/Capture: detect antigens (e.g., HBsAg, p24 antigen of HIV). 4th generation HIV ELISA: detects BOTH p24 antigen and anti-HIV antibodies simultaneously (reduces window period to ~2 weeks). High sensitivity (screening), confirm positive results with Western blot or confirmatory assay. Reference: Ananthanarayan — ELISA.
